steps.text: Understand the nature of static electricity.
Identify and assess the hazards of static electricity.
Control the hazards of static electricity.
Control static electricity in selected industrial applications.
Apply the following standards to the design and operation of the new Port Wentworth facility.
Conduct a comprehensive review of all existing Imperial Sugar Company sugar manufacturing facilities against the standards listed in recommendations and implement identified corrective actions.

Implement a corporate-wide comprehensive housekeeping program to control combustible dust accumulation that will ensure sugar dust, cornstarch dust, or other combustible dust does not accumulate to hazardous quantities on overhead horizontal surfaces, packing equipment, and floors.
Develop training materials that address combustible dust hazards and train all employees and contractors at all Imperial Sugar Company facilities.
Improve the emergency evacuation policies and procedures at the Port Wentworth facility.
Install an emergency alert (alarm) system in the facility.
Require routine emergency evacuation drills and critiques.
Contact your AO and/or other offices within OSHA as needed to get approval to sample hybrid mixtures.
For assistance with sampling hybrid mixtures contact the HRT.
Contact your AO and/or other offices within OSHA as needed to get approval to sample combustible dusts with other hazardous characteristics.
For assistance with sampling these mixtures contact the HRT.
Follow the additional guidance found in Appendix I for sampling combustible metal dusts.
Call the AO and discuss the concerns with your assistant area director (AAD) and/or area director (AD).
Consult with the RO to determine how to proceed if it is too dangerous to sample.
Decide if an imminent hazard notice may be appropriate in accordance with the FOM.
CSHOs should be collecting their own samples.
Use facility personnel to take the samples while the CSHO observes if dealing with low-MIE combustible dusts.
Obtain additional information regarding combustible dust MIEs prior to sampling.
Obtain samples necessary to support an enforcement action in a safe manner using procedures in this protocol, and in coordination with their supervisor.
Consider using the guidelines in this protocol for sampling if the dust is observed to be an extremely fine powder.
Do not collect a sample without AO and RO approval in cases of hybrid mixtures or combustible dusts with other hazards.
Notify the Regional Office in cases where HRT assistance may be necessary.
Suspend the 200 mesh (75µm) screen sample in a Hartmann stainless steel 1.2 liter explosion chamber and ignite with an electrical spark.
Record the maximum pressure and rate of pressure rise developed by the explosion if the sample explodes.
Calculate ES as the product of the maximum explosion pressure (Pmax) and the maximum rate of pressure rise (Kst), normalized to Pittsburgh coal dust.
Suspend further tests and report the sample as Class II dust if ES is greater than or equal to 0.5.
Test the sample in the 20-liter explosibility testing chamber if no explosion occurs in the Hartmann Class II chamber.
Mark out an area to be sampled and measure and record this area in field notes.
Take depth measurements in the area in order to get an average depth and record in field notes.
Collect all material in the marked area in a 1-liter sample bottle.
Send the sample bottle, along with the depth and area measurements, to the lab.
Dry the sample for 24 hours in a drying oven set at 167°F or 75°C after receiving at the SLTC lab.
Weigh the sample.
Determine the bulk density based on the volume and weight of the sample as Dry Weight / Initial Volume.

preconditions.text: If a building or building compartment contains a dust explosion hazard outside of equipment, areas shall be provided with deflagration venting to a safe area in accordance with NFPA 68.

Where an explosion hazard exists within any operating equipment greater than 8 ft3 (0.2 m3) of containing volume, the equipment shall be protected from the effects of a deflagration by deflagration venting, deflagration suppression, or deflagration pressure containment.

Before obtaining samples, ensure that any chemical manufacturer data, employer commissioned MIE testing results, or published data show that the MIE of a material is ≤ 30 mJ or is likely to be ≤ 30 mJ in the form to be sampled.
If the dust is not shown to have a MIE ≤ 30 mJ, but is an extremely fine powder, consider guidelines for sampling.
In cases of hybrid mixtures or combustible dusts with other hazards, do not collect a sample without AO and RO approval.

constraints.text: Prohibit the use of sprinkler systems and water deluge systems in all buildings that process or store combustible metals.
Wear PPE as described in the NEP.
Use of the HRT for site visits to perform sampling or other activities must be approved by the RO.
Only those familiar with the hazards and handling procedures for the dust can be used to take samples.
Potential ignition source mechanism(s) include frictional heating and/or sparking from introduction of foreign materials.
Potential ignition source mechanism(s) also include frictional heating and/or sparking from mechanical components.
Concerns regarding potential dust cloud formation mechanisms through grinding.
Routine maintenance (e.g., lubrication, proper clearances) is necessary.
Deflagration suppression and pressure containment are recommended.
Deflagration suppression and venting are necessary.
Pressure containment and inerting are necessary.
